title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: DTE profits from data centers could mean higher rates for Michigan households.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Today we're digging into how data center expansion is shaping up as a major earnings driver for DTE Energy and what that could mean for everyday ratepayers across the state.
That sounds like the kind of story that hits right in the wallet, Graham. Let's start there.
DTE's Q2 preview out today flags data-center growth as a key part of their earnings picture.
So the utility sees this as a positive for their bottom line, but how does that translate to customers?
More demand on the grid often means new infrastructure costs or higher rates passed along to households.
We've seen charges tied to similar projects already in parts of their territory, right?
Exactly. Ratepayers in those areas have already felt added fees linked to earlier builds.
How long has this tension between data centers and utilities been building in Michigan?
It's been ramping up over the past couple of years as hyperscale projects started landing in the Midwest.
Who are the main players pushing these projects forward?
Big tech firms like Oracle alongside local utilities and economic development groups.
And on the other side, who is raising the alarms about costs?
Township officials, residents, and county commissioners worried about power strain and local resources.
What does a big data center actually pull in terms of electricity?
Hundreds of megawatts in many cases, which is enough to power tens of thousands of homes.
That kind of load has to affect planning for the whole grid.
It does, and utilities like DTE have to decide whether to build new generation or transmission to keep up.
Water use is another big piece I've heard about.
Cooling systems can draw millions of gallons daily, which raises flags in areas already managing resources carefully.
How does that intersect with farmland and rural towns?
Many proposed sites sit on or near agricultural land, so there's competition for both power and water.
What kind of tax revenue are communities hoping to see in return?
Some deals include incentives or abatements that limit how much new revenue actually stays local for years.
That sounds like a trade-off residents are starting to question more openly.
It is, especially when promised jobs don't always materialize at the scale first discussed.
What's the next step for DTE customers watching this play out?
Rate cases and infrastructure filings will likely spell out how costs get allocated in the months ahead.
And for local governments debating projects right now?
They're weighing moratoria and zoning changes to buy time for better rules.
That brings us to some of the other moves happening today and yesterday.
Let's hit the quick ones. In Calhoun County today, commissioners are debating a year-long halt on new data centers.
Over concerns about electricity and whether the economic gains reach local taxpayers?
That's the core of it. A pause would delay projects drawing hundreds of megawatts and millions of gallons of water.
Battle Creek residents would feel that in both future taxes and monthly bills.
Meanwhile in Augusta Township, officials are close to a vote on zoning and tax terms for a large proposal pulling power from the same DTE area as Saline.
The outcome there could set the tone for how much new load hits ratepayers and what revenue the township actually collects.
Over in Cass County, Dowagiac residents are still dealing with noise from an operating facility's cooling systems.
Persistent humming disrupting sleep, and they're looking at legal options including possible class action.
Shifting to yesterday, Saline Township reversed course and restored Oracle's shot at a big tax break.
After earlier limits, the board flipped following company lobbying focused on jobs.
That leaves residents facing years of forgone property-tax revenue on a multibillion-dollar project.
Delta County also acted yesterday, voting a one-year ban on new proposals.
Residents there flagged noise, water, and rural grid strain, giving officials time to draft permanent zoning.
MLive launched a map yesterday showing every active fight across the state.
It tracks proposals, hearings, and moratoria by township with details on megawatts and tax deals.
Handy for anyone wanting to compare what's happening near them.
In Oakland County, Wixom kept its moratorium in place yesterday to protect against power strain and noise.
Council rejected an early end to the pause on a three-building complex.
Williamston Township shelved its zoning rules for now, leaving uncertainty for homes and farms.
And Williamstown Township delayed its ordinance until August after a hearing on setbacks, noise, water, and decommissioning.
Residents there raised wildlife and resource concerns.
So the pattern is clear: more places hitting pause or tightening rules while utilities see growth ahead.
On deck, there are no public meetings scheduled for today that we've confirmed on the calendar.
Listeners should still check the Michigan Data Center Tracker for any last-minute additions or hearing notices.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: DTE Bills Loom Larger as Data Centers Drive Utility Growth
